channel.title: Code Defence Intelligence Brief — Wed, 01 Apr 2026
channel.description: Daily cybersecurity intelligence for Wed, 01 Apr 2026.

item.title: North Korea UNC1069 compromises Axios npm package in major supply chain attack
item.description:
One of the world most trusted HTTP client libraries has been weaponized as a delivery mechanism for a cross-platform remote access trojan. This attack targets developer workstations and automated build pipelines that pull the latest versions of the Axios npm package.
On March 31‚ 2026‚ the North Korea-nexus threat actor UNC1069 compromised the maintainer account for Axios and published poisoned versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4. These releases contain a malicious dependency named plain-crypto-js that executes an obfuscated dropper via npm post-install hooks. The payload‚ WAVESHAPER.V2‚ provides persistent remote access and automates the exfiltration of environment variables‚ cloud access keys‚ and SSH tokens.
Organizations often prioritize speed over dependency integrity in their CI/CD workflows. This incident demonstrates that implicit trust in a reputable package name is a high-impact failure point. When a primary library is compromised‚ the attack surface is no longer your perimeter‚ but the verified code running inside your production infrastructure.
- Check project lockfiles for Axios versions 1.14.1 or 0.30.4 and reference to plain-crypto-js. - Revert to Axios 1.14.0 or 0.30.3 immediately and delete affected node_modules directories. - Rotate all secrets present in environment variables on any system where the poisoned versions executed. - Enforce SHA-256 hash pinning for all critical npm dependencies to prevent automated version hijacking.
Implicit trust in collaborative code-sharing communities is a systemic risk that requires architectural hardening rather than simple patching. #CodeDefence #SupplyChain #Axios #UNC1069

item.title: Google issues emergency Chrome update for zero-day exploited in drive-by attacks
item.description:
Attackers are leveraging a high-severity memory corruption flaw in the Chromium engine to achieve remote code execution on Windows and macOS systems. This zero-day enables drive-by compromises where systems are infected simply by visiting a malicious or compromised website.
CVE-2026-2441 is a use-after-free vulnerability in the Chrome CSS rendering engine. By abusing how the engine processes font feature values‚ attackers can manipulate application memory to execute arbitrary code. Google confirmed this flaw has been under active exploitation since February. While execution is currently limited to the browser sandbox‚ it provides the necessary foundation for full system takeover when chained with a secondary sandbox escape.
The modern browser is the most volatile entry point in the enterprise fleet because it is designed to execute untrusted code from the internet by default. User education is insufficient for drive-by attacks; the solution must be technical and immediate to mitigate the risk of session hijacking.
- Force update @[Google](urn:li:organization:1441) Chrome to version 145.0.7632.75 or higher across all managed endpoints. - Enable site isolation and hardware-enforced stack protection via Group Policy or MDM. - Utilize browser isolation or zero-trust web gateways for high-risk users to neutralize web-based exploits. - Audit network logs for anomalous outbound traffic originating from browser processes to known malicious C2 IPs.
Browser engines are high-value targets for initial access brokers because they bypass traditional network-layer defenses. #CodeDefence #Chrome #ZeroDay #PatchManagement

item.title: TeamPCP pivots to active extortion using stolen Trivy and Checkmarx CI/CD secrets
item.description:
The threat actor group TeamPCP has transitioned from initial credential harvesting to large-scale extortion of multi-billion-dollar organizations. This follows the systemic compromise of the Trivy and Checkmarx security scanners used in thousands of enterprise build pipelines.
Tracked under CVE-2026-33634‚ the group utilized compromised service accounts to push malicious code into official GitHub Action tags. Researchers at @[Wiz](urn:li:organization:27083049) report that TeamPCP is now collaborating with the Lapsus$ group to monetize approximately 300 GB of exfiltrated CI/CD secrets. These secrets provide direct access to cloud providers‚ SaaS environments‚ and internal codebases.
When security tools are turned into weapons‚ they become the most efficient vehicle for data exfiltration because they already possess the high-level permissions required to traverse the network. This highlights the operational danger of maintaining long-lived secrets in CI/CD runners.
- Rotate all AWS keys‚ Azure tokens‚ and database credentials present in environments where Trivy or Checkmarx ran in the last 14 days. - Transition to OIDC-based short-lived credentials for all cloud provider interactions in CI/CD. - Apply all vendor mitigations for Trivy and ensure all GitHub Actions are pinned to full SHA-256 hashes. - Monitor cloud provider logs for anomalous service principal activity or unauthorized resource creation.
The compromise of security tooling requires a forensic assumption of full infrastructure exposure rather than a simple patch cycle. #CodeDefence #Trivy #TeamPCP #DevSecOps

item.title: Attackers exploit critical Langflow AI framework RCE within hours of disclosure
item.description:
Threat actors are weaponizing a critical code injection flaw in the Langflow AI framework to hijack agent workflows and steal sensitive environment data. Active exploitation was observed less than a day after the vulnerability became public.
CVE-2026-33017 stems from a default setting that leaves an API endpoint unauthenticated in versions prior to 1.9.0. This allows attackers to submit malicious workflow data containing embedded Python code. Successful exploitation grants the ability to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the Langflow service account‚ leading to the theft of LLM provider API keys and database credentials.
The rapid pace of AI adoption is outpacing traditional security oversight‚ resulting in "shadow AI" deployments with insecure default configurations. Attackers are operationalizing automation to find and exploit these frameworks as soon as they are connected to the public internet.
- Immediately upgrade Langflow to version 1.9.0 or higher across all development and production environments. - Place all AI orchestration and agent frameworks behind a VPN or Zero Trust gateway to prevent public API exposure. - Enforce strict identity-based access controls for all internal API endpoints in the AI stack. - Monitor for anomalous Python process executions originating from AI service accounts.
AI infrastructure represents a new high-value target for unauthenticated remote code execution. #CodeDefence #Langflow #AISecurity #CISA
